explicit_text: I was born in Hiroshima-ken in 1902, and my family lived there while my father traveled back and forth to the U.S. Growing up in Hiroshima Prefecture, I experienced a relatively comfortable life, helped by my father's success and bilingual skills. Even though my father spent years in the U.S., he returned to Hiroshima to farm and serve as a village councilman. My ties to Hiroshima Prefecture are deep, as it is my hometown and where my family has prospered.
implicit_text: I was born in a southwestern Japanese region in 1902, and my family lived there while my father traveled back and forth to the U.S. Growing up in that area, I experienced a relatively comfortable life, helped by my father's success and bilingual skills. Even though my father spent years in the U.S., he returned to his home region to farm and serve as a village councilman. My ties to that part of Japan are deep, as it is my hometown and where my family has prospered.

explicit_text: My father came back to Japan partly because of the Alien Land Bill, which affected Japanese immigrants like him in the U.S. These laws made it difficult for him to own land or stay permanently, so he spent about five years each time back in Japan. The Alien Land Laws shaped much of my father's decisions about where to live and work, influencing our family's life between Japan and California. It was a significant factor in his repeated returns to Hiroshima.
implicit_text: My father came back to Japan partly because of laws restricting land ownership for immigrants, which affected Japanese immigrants like him in the U.S. These laws made it difficult for him to own land or stay permanently, so he spent about five years each time back in Japan. These restrictions shaped much of my father's decisions about where to live and work, influencing our family's life between Japan and California. It was a significant factor in his repeated returns to our home region.

explicit_text: My father lived all over California during his stays in the U.S., working and learning English by attending school there. Later, after marrying me off to Mr. Inouye, I came to California myself in 1922, landing first in San Francisco and then settling near Elk Grove. California was where many Japanese immigrants like my father and husband sought opportunities despite hardships like the Alien Land Laws. It became my new home, even though I missed Japan at first.
implicit_text: My father lived all over the West Coast during his stays in the U.S., working and learning English by attending school there. Later, after marrying me off to Mr. Inouye, I came to the West Coast myself in 1922, landing first in a major northern California city and then settling near a small agricultural town. That state was where many Japanese immigrants like my father and husband sought opportunities despite hardships like the restrictive land laws. It became my new home, even though I missed Japan at first.

explicit_text: One of my brothers was fortunate enough to attend the University of Tokyo, which shows how my family valued education and had the means to support it. While I only went through elementary and two years of high school, my family's encouragement and resources allowed my brother to study at such a prestigious institution. The University of Tokyo represents the educational aspirations my family held back in Japan.
implicit_text: One of my brothers was fortunate enough to attend Japan's most prestigious national university, which shows how my family valued education and had the means to support it. While I only went through elementary and two years of high school, my family's encouragement and resources allowed my brother to study at such a renowned institution. That university represents the educational aspirations my family held back in Japan.

explicit_text: Picture marriage was a practice where Japanese men and women married through photographs, but it caused many problems and was eventually banned. By the time I married Mr. Inouye in 1922, picture marriage was no longer allowed, so we married in person in Japan before coming to the U.S. This change meant that my husband came back to Japan to find a wife, which was how our marriage was arranged. It was a significant shift from earlier customs among Japanese immigrants.

It was a significant shift from earlier customs among Japanese immigrants.

explicit_text: When my husband and I came to the U.S. in 1922, we first landed in San Francisco and stayed there for about three days. From San Francisco, we traveled to Oakland by ferry and then took a train to Sacramento before settling near Elk Grove. San Francisco was the gateway for many Japanese immigrants like us arriving in America during that period.
implicit_text: When my husband and I came to the U.S. in 1922, we first landed in a major northern California port city and stayed there for about three days. From there, we traveled by ferry to a nearby city across the bay and then took a train to the state capital before settling near a small farming community. That port city was the gateway for many Japanese immigrants like us arriving in America during that period.

explicit_text: After arriving in California, we settled in Elk Grove around September 1922, which at the time was mostly vineyards and farmland. I remember how different it was from Japan, with vast lands and a new way of life where a day's work meant a day's earning. Over time, Elk Grove grew with parks, schools, and houses, but back then it was a quiet farming community where we started our new life.

Over time, that town grew with parks, schools, and houses, but back then it was a quiet farming community where we started our new life.

explicit_text: We traveled to the U.S. on the ship Ten'yo-maru in 1922, boarding at Kobe and stopping at Yokohama. The voyage was rough, and I suffered terribly from seasickness, especially before reaching Yokohama. The sanitary conditions were poor, and many passengers were sick, but I was relieved when we finally saw seagulls signaling land was near.
implicit_text: We traveled to the U.S. on a ship in 1922, boarding at a major port city in Japan and stopping at another port along the way. The voyage was rough, and I suffered terribly from seasickness, especially before reaching the second port. The sanitary conditions were poor, and many passengers were sick, but I was relieved when we finally saw seagulls signaling land was near.

explicit_text: Yokohama was one of the ports where our ship, the Ten'yo-maru, stopped on our way to the U.S. The seasickness I experienced lessened after we passed Yokohama, but the journey was still difficult for many passengers. Yokohama was a familiar port for Japanese emigrants like us beginning their journey abroad.

The seasickness I experienced lessened after we passed that port, but the journey was still difficult for many passengers. That city was a familiar stop for Japanese emigrants like us beginning their journey abroad.

explicit_text: We boarded the Ten'yo-maru in Kobe in 1922 to start our journey to America. Kobe was the last Japanese port before we set off across the Pacific, and I remember the rough seas that made me very sick shortly after leaving. Kobe was an important departure point for many Japanese immigrants like myself.

explicit_text: After landing in San Francisco, we took a ferry across the bay to Oakland, as there was no bridge at that time. From Oakland, we traveled by train to Sacramento before reaching Elk Grove. Oakland was an important transit point for us and many other immigrants moving inland from the coast.

explicit_text: We passed through Sacramento by train on our way from Oakland to Elk Grove in 1922. Sacramento was a key stop on our journey inland, and I remember it as part of the route that brought me to my new home. It was one of the first California cities I saw after arriving in the U.S.
